data.title: Palantir: No Rational Reason To Own It Besides Momentum
data.description:
About six months ago, I wrote a piece on Palantir (PLTR) where I highlighted a strategy based on the riding-the-bull thesis without being thrown off, which involved following a strategy based on reducing exposure during high volatility and weak trends, significantly outperforming just by holding the stock. The aim was to avoid the painful drawdowns that an absurdly rich stock like PLTR could eventually offer.

And in short, the way to survive an extreme convexity thesis is not to ignore risk, but to manage the trend regime.

I would argue that this point has become even more relevant now, in the midst of an environment in which exactly the same symptoms that motivated that earlier thesis are replicating. In the absence of a reliable "fair multiple" and when inaccurate projections tend to depend on still distant options, the only real actionable instrument for investing falls to positioning.

That said, under the argument that Palantir should be treated as a momentum discipline asset, not as a conventional valuation story, what we see today in terms of (1) divergence of moving averages; (2) short-term stress; (3) absence of capitulation; and (4) the back and forth of flows reinforces the playbook of repeating the trend, reducing positions in weak regimes and increasing them only when the tape confirms recovery.

Everyone's Right, Everyone's Uncomfortable

Setting the stage, Palantir's fundamentals today can be considered insane, as can its valuation. Therefore, the only honest way I see to frame Palantir now would be through positioning and momentum.

As a quick recap, Palantir's 3Q25 brought the following highlights: (1) Rule of 40 at 114% (revenue growth 64% YoY + operating margins at 50%); (2) U.S. commercial growing 121% YoY, and U.S. overall growing 77% YoY; (3) massive FCF at $1.1 billion and buyback firepower ($373.8 million repurchased in the last twelve months, but mostly to offset SBC dilution).

According to the sell-side view, the interpretation of the moment is basically based on "best software company" at the moment, but trading at 87x forward sales and 224x forward earnings, this is almost nonsensical. This shows that no one disagrees with the business, but that the whole debate revolves around how much future growth is already baked into the price.

This leads me to classify Palantir's thesis as primarily a momentum asset, not a valuation asset.

The Valuation Problem

As Seeking Alpha's rates show, Palantir's multiples are so absurd that they trade ~5x richer than high-quality infra software peers. This territory is so unprecedented that there is no way to anchor a "fair" multiple in this company's thesis.

Today's valuation is based on multiple years of 50%+ revenue growth and at least 50% operating margins—and obviously a macro resilient enough to support this outlook.

This undoubtedly causes a "battleground" dynamic where, on one side, Palantir is labeled as the best AI operator worth a premium, and on the other, this is a fantastic company but trading at a fantasy price. In reality, I believe that both sides may be overexposing themselves, just handicapping crowd psychology.

In my last thesis, written on Palantir, I outlined what could be embedded in a $1 trillion valuation for the company, based on the capitalization of options in the thesis that can best explain a 200x-plus multiple.

Quickly put into context, these optionalities were based on five key points: (1) general-purpose corporate AI, with AIP at the heart of the thesis being universally adopted among enterprises; (2) the expansion of government contracts in emerging countries and NATO; (3) capitalization under new vertical sectors such as healthcare and finance; (4) deepen internationalization mainly in Europe and Asia; and (5) partnerships and acquisitions potentially integrating Palantir with other software.

Considering the respective addressable markets for each of these initiatives, and putting the variables of cost of execution, time of realization, uncertainty of a payoff, and a reasonable risk-free rate with the current one, I had estimated $1.1 trillion of extra options to be incorporated into Palantir's thesis, already over what the company delivers today—with more than half of that amount linked to an accessible TAM for AIP by 2034 in the trillions of dollars as an average baseline. As a reference, third-party firms say that AI in general could add something like $15-16 tri/year to world GDP by 2030.

Of course, this is an assumption, with a high dose of speculation, and potentially adjusted to perfection. But the purpose of showing this path is precisely an exercise in how one could justify (or not justify) a monstrously high multiple for an unprecedented and unanchored thesis.

Momentum as the Real Thesis

If it's practically impossible to reach a common denominator to justify valuations convincingly, let's get down to the nitty-gritty of Palantir's thesis and the real reason why investors keep buying the thesis and sustaining the high premium: momentum.

Since momentum will tend to guide investors in and out of this thesis above all else, let's look at the current state of the trend.

Palantir recently hit all-time highs of ~$207 per share and suffered a pullback to $160s-not necessarily justified by fundamentals, as 3Q25 earnings were pristine, to be clear. The problem is that in the short term, the risk of a choppy regime occurs as valuation digests. Keep in mind that flows and narrative drive as much as fundamentals, and a PLTR is a "story vehicle" for AI.

With that, let's split into sub-profiles: early bulls vs. late chasers.

For those who are in from much lower (e.g., teens), they are probably sitting on four-digit percentage gains, and I believe the risk of giving back too much if the regime changes. Selling covered calls after big rallies or monitoring short-term moving averages and trimming on vertical spikes could be the case.

But essentially, I would only consider zeroing the position in a scenario of price breaking below the 50-day SMA and (1) failing to regain momentum in 2 to 3 weeks; and (2) volume on red days being higher than on Green days. In theory, this would be the biggest sign that big money is distributing, not accumulating, and every major drawdown tends to start that way. Or, if Palantir ever closes 2-3 weeks below the 200-day SMA, that would show that it's no longer in a secular uptrend.

As the chart below shows, Palantir's price at the time of writing, at $161.64, is below the 20-day and 50-day SMA, proving to be a no-buy zone for a volatile stock. For stubborn bulls, buying in this setup would be fighting with momentum, and the chance of success is low. Generally, good buy setups move above the 20-day SMA, or else when the 20-day SMA crosses above the 50-day SMA (confirmation).

Note also that the volume (57.88 million) and the 30-day average volume (57.20 million) show a normalization, showing no fear of capitulation yet, and no strong accumulation—arguably good for building a base, but not a buy trigger.

So, I would say that ideally, the perfect time to go long on Palantir for late chasers mitigating risk, would be if (1) price closes above the 20-day SMA (first sign of sellers exhaustion); (2) price closes above the 50-day SMA (confirmation); (3) volume spikes above ~70 million, indicating smart money stepping in. It gets even better if a position is built up gradually according to the SMA's unfolding as described.

Conclusion

I believe that for those who are carrying Palantir with some relevant gain, the worst mistake now would be to get rid of the position out of emotion, just as it would be irresponsible to ignore signs of a regime change.

The current moment, technically, is not a "sell everything" period, but one that requires some level of monitoring discipline. The long-term trend remains intact, which supports a structural bull case, but the short term is weakened—below the 20- and 50-day SMAs, with no signs of volume re-acceleration.

A zero-position scenario would only make sense under a technical confirmation of institutional distribution, breaking the 50-day with failure to return, and then breaking the 200-day. Until then, tactical trims, covered calls, and gradual reductions make more sense than an early full exit.

But overall, I would summarize the thesis by pointing out that at the moment, there is no sensible reason in the short to medium term to own Palantir besides momentum-which is exactly why investors need a clear playbook, whether it's long, flat, or tempted to bet against it.

data.title: John Hancock Emerging Markets Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The fund's benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, posted a robust gain in the third quarter.
  • The fund underperformed the benchmark.
  • Positioning in Indonesia and Taiwan detracted from results.

Market review and outlook

Emerging-market stocks performed very well and strongly outpaced developed markets in the quarter. A favorable backdrop of positive global growth, falling interest rates, and a general lack of negative news flow fueled a surge in investors' appetite for risk. While all regions posted gains, Asia led the way thanks to impressive returns for China, South Korea, and Taiwan. The Latin America region made a further contribution to index returns.

Contributors and detractors

An overweight in Indonesia, together with stock selection in the country, detracted from relative performance. Indonesian stocks came under pressure from civil unrest, as well as political and fiscal uncertainty, leading to a negative return for the fund's position in Bank Mandiri Tbk. Stock selection in Taiwan also hurt performance. The shortfall was largely due to a position in eMemory Technology, Inc., which reported weaker-than-expected results. Stock selection in Brazil was also an additional detractor. The e-commerce giant MercadoLibre, Inc. (MELI), which introduced extremely generous delivery options to defend its market position—a decision that adversely affected the company's profitability—was the primary detractor.

Selection in South Korea contributed to performance. The country's technology sector was buoyed by continued enthusiasm about the AI trade, as well as the potential for improving corporate governance. Samsung Electronics Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) was the key driver of the fund's positive performance in this market. An overweight in Greece also contributed thanks to strong results from our positions in the banking sector. Outside of these two countries, the Chinese stocks Contemporary Amperex Technology Co., Ltd., Tencent Holdings Ltd. (OTCPK:TCEHY), and Trip.com Group Ltd. (TCOM) were among top contributors during the period.

Portfolio changes

Our stock selection process remains grounded in thorough bottom-up analysis that seeks to identify companies that can generate earnings growth despite top-down challenges.

Tariffs remain a key consideration for emerging-market investors. We think it's reasonable to expect that GDP growth and aggregate earnings will slow across the asset class, not least due to over-ordering ahead of the imposition of tariffs. Many companies are unable to pass the full cost of tariffs on to their customers, which could have an adverse effect on profits.

China remains the focal point of U.S. trade policy, and we may see clarity on final tariff levels before year end. Policy support, positive sentiment surrounding China's homegrown advances in AI, and undemanding valuations have combined to fuel a market recovery. However, slower global trade could hinder growth in the nation's economy. The trade issue could present an interesting opportunity in Brazil since the effect of the reciprocal tariffs should be small, and potential rate cuts will likely be far more influential for growth. Similarly, India has the fiscal, monetary, and external buffers to manage any shocks. We therefore believe India's long-term structural growth story remains intact, albeit with some short-term challenges.

Despite the headwind of slower global growth and the possible risk to earnings, we remain optimistic about the outlook for emerging-market stocks. A number of countries have already started cutting interest rates, which helps economic growth and lowers the cost of capital. Further rate cuts in the United States—should they materialize—and continued weakness in the U.S. dollar can also be expected to be supportive for the asset class. Still, the emerging markets continue to trade at a compelling discount relative to their developed-market peers. In our view, this indicates a broad range of opportunities for individual stock selection.
